label: Standard — memoir author on a writing podcast

input.guest_background: A memoirist who published her first book about growing up in a family of competitive chess players. The book was a surprise bestseller. She previously worked as a journalist and has spoken about the ethical challenges of writing about living family members.
input.episode_focus: The ethics and craft of writing about real people you love — when memoir becomes family exposure
input.show_tone: Thoughtful literary audience, conversational but not casual, host asks substantive questions
input.specific_moments: She has said in a prior interview that her mother didn't speak to her for three months after reading the manuscript

input.guest_background: A former professional chef who left restaurant work to become a food safety consultant. He now advises restaurants on health code compliance and has strong opinions about why restaurant kitchens are more dangerous than people realize.
input.episode_focus: What really happens behind the kitchen door — food safety truths the industry doesn't advertise
input.show_tone: Curious general audience, casual and conversational

label: European context — Dutch urban planner on a cities podcast

input.guest_background: A Dutch urban planner and professor who has advised multiple European cities on car-free city center design. He led the planning team for a major Dutch city's car-free zone implementation and has written extensively about why the Dutch model of urban cycling infrastructure works and why it fails when exported to other countries without adaptation.
input.episode_focus: Why copying Dutch cycling infrastructure doesn't work — and what cities should actually do instead
input.show_tone: Smart general audience interested in cities and design, curious and evidence-based, no jargon
input.topics_to_avoid: He has asked not to discuss the political controversy around one specific city's implementation
input.prior_work: His book 'Beyond the Bike Lane: Why Urban Cycling Is About Cities, Not Bicycles' published last year

input.guest_background: A climate migration researcher who is also personally a climate refugee — her family lost their home in Honduras to Hurricane Eta. She now studies the policy frameworks that failed her family. She is comfortable discussing her personal experience but has said she does not want to be treated primarily as a victim.
input.episode_focus: How climate migration policy looks different when the researcher has lived it
input.show_tone: Serious public affairs audience, respectful, evidence-based
input.specific_moments: She has publicly criticised the UN refugee framework for not recognising climate migrants

input.guest_background: An economist who argues that remote work is a net negative for the economy, productivity, and worker wellbeing. His position is backed by data but is deeply unpopular with the podcast's audience (90% remote workers). He is articulate and experienced in hostile interviews.
input.episode_focus: Steel-manning the case against remote work — the data, the arguments, and the blind spots
input.show_tone: Intellectually honest business audience that values being challenged, not just confirmed
input.specific_moments: His viral paper showed that remote workers are promoted 35% less often than in-office peers, even controlling for performance

input.guest_background: A Japanese master craftsman (Living National Treasure) who has spent 50 years making traditional lacquerware. He speaks minimal English. The interview will be conducted through a translator.
input.episode_focus: What 50 years of daily practice teaches you about mastery, patience, and the value of slow work
input.show_tone: Contemplative, cultural, mindful — audience interested in craft and philosophy
input.specific_moments: He reportedly trains one apprentice at a time and has only produced three in his career. His rejection of mass production in favour of 6-month-per-piece craftsmanship is the central tension.
